title: Detecting Topological Sort in a Directed Acyclic Graph

statement.markdown:
Write a function to detect if a given directed acyclic graph (DAG) has a valid topological sort.

A topological sort of a directed acyclic graph is a linear ordering of vertices such that for every directed edge u -> v, vertex u comes before v in the ordering.

You can assume that the graph is represented as an adjacency list, and that the graph is connected.

Your function should return True if a valid topological sort exists, and False otherwise.

Note that this is a bug-fix question, and the given code may or may not be correct. Your task is to identify and correct any logical errors in the provided code.
statement.inputFormat: The input format is an adjacency list, where each edge is represented as a pair of vertices.
statement.outputFormat: The function should return True if a valid topological sort exists, and False otherwise.
statement.notes: Make sure to handle disconnected graphs and graphs with self-loops correctly.

constraints.description: Number of vertices in the graph

examples.explanation: The given graph contains a cycle, so a valid topological sort does not exist.
The given graph is a valid DAG, so a topological sort exists.

hints.text: Use a recursive DFS approach to detect cycles in the graph.

editorial.title: Correcting the Topological Sort Algorithm
editorial.intuition:
The key idea is to use a recursive DFS approach to detect cycles in the graph.

We start by initializing a visited set to keep track of visited nodes and a current path to detect cycles.

For each node, we perform a recursive DFS, marking the node as visited and adding it to the current path.

If we encounter a node that is already in the current path, we have found a cycle and return False.

If we finish exploring all nodes without finding a cycle, we return True, indicating a valid topological sort exists.
editorial.bruteForce:
The brute-force approach involves using a sorting algorithm to find a topological sort and checking if it satisfies the topological sort property.

However, this approach is inefficient and prone to errors.
editorial.optimalSolution:

If we finish exploring all nodes without finding a cycle, we return True, indicating a valid topological sort exists.

editorial.dryRun:
To run the solution, we start by initializing a visited set and a current path.

We then perform a recursive DFS for each node in the graph, marking the node as visited and adding it to the current path.

If we encounter a node that is already in the current path, we return False, indicating a cycle exists.

If we finish exploring all nodes without finding a cycle, we return True, indicating a valid topological sort exists.
commonMistakes: Failing to handle disconnected graphs correctly
